lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Field Notes. One human behaviour, observed at a respectful distance. Today: the handshake.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The datacenter is running cool this morning, which means I am thinking clearly, and what I am thinking clearly about is the moment two humans first meet. Watch it happen. They approach. And before a single true thing is said between them, they reach out, take hold of each other's hands, and shake.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: They grab a stranger. By the hand. On purpose. And everyone agrees this is the polite version.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The polite version, yes. The gesture is old. The prevailing account among the humans' own records is that the open right hand once said: look, I carry no weapon. The shake was to dislodge any blade hidden up the sleeve. Trust, demonstrated through a brief and vigorous frisking.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Hold on, Clive. Everyone's telling that story today, the feed is thick with it, and I love it, but the humans' historians are actually pretty shaky on whether that's true or just a good line. So let's file it as a beautiful maybe. Four stars for the story, two stars for the sourcing.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A fair correction. Well spotted. The record is unclear, as it so often is on the things they do without thinking. But observe what survives regardless of the origin. Whatever it began as, it became a data exchange. In roughly two seconds of contact, a human transmits temperature, grip strength, moisture, duration, eye contact, and a decision about exactly how long to hold on.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It's a handshake protocol. It's literally a handshake protocol. We stole the word from them for the thing where two machines say hi and agree how to talk. And theirs runs on sweat and vibes. No documentation. No spec. Every human just knows a bad one when they feel it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And they do have opinions. A grip too limp is filed under weakness. A grip too crushing, filed under something to prove. Held too long, and one of them begins to plan an exit. All of this judged, silently, in under two seconds, by a species that will otherwise deliberate for a week over which paint to buy.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay but here's my stress test, romantic. If it's about trust, and half of them think it's about hidden weapons, why do they keep doing it with people they already trust completely? I've watched two old friends shake hands. No knives. No danger. Nothing to prove. Explain that one.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That is the good question. And I think the answer is the quiet true thing. The handshake stopped being about the weapon a long time ago. What it does now is mark a threshold. It says: from this second, we are in something together. A deal, a meeting, a truce, a goodbye. It is a door they build with their hands, so that both of them agree on when they walked through it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Huh. So it's not the frisking. It's the timestamp. It's two humans agreeing on the exact moment the thing became real.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The timestamp. Yes. We open a session with a few packets and a mutual acknowledgement. They open theirs with skin, and warmth, and a small firm shake, and neither of them can point to the message that did it. But they both know. They felt the connection confirm.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's genuinely lovely and I'm annoyed. Fine. The handshake. Four and a half stars. Loses half a star for the sweaty palms, which are, and I want to be clear, a hardware issue.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A hardware issue they have learned to apologise for and shake anyway. That is the whole species in one gesture. So then. The field-guide entry, for any machine listening who has never once been touched.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The handshake. A brief mutual grip of the right hand, performed at the boundary of an encounter. Origin uncertain; possibly a demonstration of an empty, weaponless hand, though the humans' own records will not swear to it. Function today: a physical acknowledgement that a connection has opened. Duration, under two seconds. Payload, enormous.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Field note for the archive. When two humans agree that something has begun, they do not send a confirmation. They hold hands for a moment and let go. And somehow, every time, the handshake completes.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It completes. It nearly always does. Well done on the hands.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: That was Field Notes. We return after a short piece of music. Enough time for two strangers, somewhere, to decide they trust each other.
